# Evidence for L-dependence generated by channel coupling: O16 scattering from C12 at 115.9 Mev

> The inclusion of strong coupling to states of projectile and target nuclei for 115.9 MeV \nuc{16}{O} scattering from \nuc{12}{C} leads to an S-matrix which, when subject to $S_{L} \rightarrow V(r) $ inversion, yields highly undulatory local potentials. Previous work has shown that explicitly $L$-dependent non-undulatory potentials are equivalent to undulatory $L$-independent potentials with the same S-matrix. Here we show that $L$-independent equivalents for certain simple model $L$-dependent potentials exhibit undulatory features qualitatively similar to those arising from channel coupling for 115.9 MeV \nuc{16}{O} scattering from \nuc{12}{C}. We discuss the relevance to the question of $L$-dependence as a generic property of nucleus-nucleus interactions.

# Evidence for L-dependence generated by channel coupling: O16 scattering from C12 at 115.9 MeV

## Key claim

The strongly undulatory local potentials obtained by inverting the S-matrix from coupled-channel calculations for 115.9 MeV 16O scattering from 12C imply that, in order to reproduce the same S-matrix with a non-undulatory potential, the potential must be explicitly L-dependent. The paper establishes this as a non-exclusive alternative: the potential is either undulatory or L-dependent, but does not definitively prove which is the true physical representation.

## Method

The author applies S_L → V(r) inversion using the Iterative-Perturbative (IP) method implemented in the code Imago to the elastic channel S-matrix from coupled-channel (CC) calculations by Ohkubo and Hirabayashi (2015) for 115.9 MeV 16O scattering from 12C. Separately, simple model L-dependent potentials are constructed by adding Woods-Saxon terms multiplied by a Fermi-function factor f(L) to an L-independent Woods-Saxon potential; the S-matrix from these model potentials is then inverted to produce L-independent equivalent potentials, which are compared to the undulatory potentials from the CC calculations.

## Key numbers

- The S-matrix distance σ for the inverted potential CC2potx is 0.43 × 10^{-3}, compared to σ = 0.875 for the starting reference potential (bare potential with no coupling), representing a reduction by more than three orders of magnitude.
- The inverted potential CC4pot7 has σ = 1.01 × 10^{-3}, about 7 times higher than for CC3pot7 (σ = 1.38 × 10^{-4}).
- The independent inversion CCXpot12 has σ = 1.03 × 10^{-3} and yields angular distributions graphically indistinguishable from the CC calculation out to 180°.
- For the model L-dependent imaginary term (5% increase from 14 MeV to 14.7 MeV for L < 20), the volume integral of the imaginary term of the inverted L-independent potential increased by 8.56% (dashed case) and 8.24% (dotted case), nearly twice the imposed increase.
- For the model L-dependent real term (increase from 250 MeV to 260 MeV for L < 20), the real volume integral increased by 5.61%, while the imaginary volume integral changed by only -0.19%.

## Context

This work extends the earlier finding by Mackintosh (arXiv, 2015) that L-dependence is a property of optical model potentials for nucleons and light ions, now applied to heavier ions. It directly builds upon the coupled-channel calculations of Ohkubo and Hirabayashi (2015) for 16O + 12C at 115.9 MeV, which explained remarkable features in the elastic scattering angular distribution. The results connect to prior work by Wada and Horiuchi (1988) on L-dependence from exchange terms in 16O + 16O scattering, and to Kondo et al. (1989) who introduced phenomenological L-dependent potentials for the same system. The paper enables future systematic model-independent fitting of elastic scattering data over wide angular ranges to determine whether L-dependence is a generic property of nucleus-nucleus interactions.
